notes.note: Rendering the Greek euangelia, "good tidings" (the word later adopted by Christianity as "gospel"); Cicero opens with it half-mockingly to report a courtroom acquittal.

notes.anchor: Valerius has been acquitted, with Hortensius defending
notes.note: An obscure trial of 60-59 BC: a Valerius was acquitted with the great orator Q. Hortensius Hortalus as defence counsel. Cicero suggests the case was fixed ("donated to the son of Aulus").

notes.anchor: donated to the son of Aulus
notes.note: "The son of Aulus" (Auli filius) is an unidentified figure to whom the verdict was reckoned a favor; the periphrasis names a man by his father's praenomen, a Roman shorthand. His identity is disputed.

"Epicrates" (Greek, "the powerful") is a code-name in the Cicero-Atticus correspondence, taken by most editors to mean Pompey; "wanton" hints at some compromising softness. The foppish "stockings and white-bound headbands" reinforce the jibe.

notes.anchor: know that you find fault with the
notes.note: Xenophon's Cyropaedia, the idealized education of Cyrus the Great; Cicero jokes that his narrow villa windows follow its prescriptions. The pun runs into the next sentence, where "Cyrus" is his architect, not the Persian king.

notes.anchor: Cyrus said that with broad apertures
notes.note: Cyrus the architect (a Greek builder employed by Cicero and Atticus), punningly sharing the name of Xenophon's Cyrus; he advised that narrow windows framed the garden views more sweetly.

notes.anchor: For if we saw by emanations
notes.note: Cicero parodies optical theory: the Epicurean view that sight works by thin films (eidola) streaming off objects, versus the Stoic/Platonic view of visual rays (aktines) issuing from the eye. He sides jokingly with the ray-theory to defend his windows.

notes.anchor: I argue Socratically on each side
notes.note: The Socratic method of arguing both sides of a question (in utramque partem) before settling on the preferable view (Greek ten areskousan, "the pleasing one"); Cicero applies it to his own political dilemma.

notes.anchor: we must bravely resist the agrarian law
notes.note: Caesar's land-distribution bill of 59 BC; Cicero weighs three responses (resist, keep quiet, or help) as he decides how to position himself toward the consul and his coalition.

notes.anchor: going off to Solonium or Antium
notes.note: Solonium was a district and estate near Lanuvium south of Rome, and Antium a coastal resort; both stand here for political withdrawal into private leisure.
L. Cornelius Balbus of Gades, Caesar's confidential agent and man of business, who came sounding out Cicero on Caesar's behalf; Cicero clarifies which "Cornelius" he means.

notes.anchor: would give pains to join Crassus with Pompey
notes.note: An early signal of the coalition (the so-called First Triumvirate) of Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus, who had long been at odds; Balbus reports Caesar's intent to reconcile them.

notes.anchor: my own concluding sentence in book three
notes.note: The closing lines of book 3 of Cicero's verse epic De Consulatu Suo, in which the Muse Calliope counsels him to hold to the path of virtue and "the praises of the good."

notes.anchor: When this was set down for me by Calliope herself
notes.note: Calliope, the Muse of epic poetry, who in Cicero's De Consulatu Suo delivers the exhortation to political virtue; he treats her words as a binding personal oracle.

notes.anchor: let us save these things for our walks at the Compitalia
notes.note: The Compitalia was a moveable Roman festival of the crossroads-gods (Lares Compitales), held in early January; Cicero proposes to talk politics with Atticus during the holiday strolls.

notes.anchor: Bring me the book of Theophrastus
notes.note: Theophrastus of Eresus, Aristotle's successor as head of the Peripatetic school; Cicero asks Atticus to fetch one of his treatises from the library of his brother Quintus.
